questions.text: Our safety goal is:

options.c: To reduce the number of accidents
options.d: OSHA does not require us to have a safety goal

questions.text: When lifting, a safe practice is to use your ________, rather than your back.
Working safely begins with an excellent safety ________ where you believe that all accidents can be ________.
List the three actions that can improve safety:
Which of the following is NOT a common rule for preventing accidents:

options.a: Never place your hands or any part of your body in the die area.
options.b: Never try to beat, cheat, or modify any point of operation safeguarding.
options.c: Follow all safety rules and regulations for your job and area.
options.d: Don't remove scrap or material from a die with your hands.
options.e: None of the above — they all prevent accidents.

questions.text: What is our housekeeping goal? (describe in your own words)
The responsibility for housekeeping belongs with ________.
Where do the following types of scrap get dumped?
Metal scrap must not be ________ together.
Used mop water must be disposed of:
The best housekeeping technique is to ________ messes from occurring.
We want to create a first impression that says that we produce ________ with the same attention to detail and quality as we do our ________.
Housekeeping activities prepare the plant for our ________ customers — those who follow us on the next shift.
Each of us must look for and ________ things that cause a mess.
Any loose tooling components or shims must be ________ to the correct tool so that it does not become lost.
The old coolant must be dumped in the ________.
While cleaning the press you must always look for indications that something broke during the last run.
Measuring and recording documentation measurements takes place ________ pulling the tools.

checks.statement_en: Using standard equipment, demonstrate safety and continuous housekeeping practices in a production environment, following the items listed on the performance check.

items.text: Adhered to all safety policies, rules, and guidelines.
Consistently demonstrated safe working practices on the job.
Informed the appropriate person or corrected unsafe conditions or situations.
Followed the rules of safe lifting when lifting or moving objects.
Worked without creating fingerprints and spots of oil and grease.
Kept the floor and benches free of scrap, paper, garbage, and oil spills.
Replaced brooms, dustpans, mops, mop buckets, tools, and equipment to the designated spot after use.
Identified items that create housekeeping problems and took corrective action.
Cleaned tools and equipment after use.
Garbage cans have been emptied into the proper hopper and returned to their designated spot.
Scrap and loose parts have been cleaned from inside the press and dumped in the correct container.
Dumped paper, bands, and other clutter after coil changes.
All rags have been picked up and placed in the appropriate spot.
Kept benches organized and free of clutter.
Mop room is clean and all equipment returned to its designated spot.
Used clean containers for steel scrap.
Used gloves when handling sharp or dangerous objects.
Disposed of used mop water in the proper spot.
Empty tool carts have been cleaned and returned to their designated spot.
Department is neat and organized, with all items in their proper place.

checks.title_en: Pulling Tools and Cleaning the Press
checks.statement_en: Using standard equipment, pull the tools and clean the press so that the standards found on the checklist below have been completed.

items.text: Removed all scrap and dumped into proper containers.
Correctly measured and recorded all documentation measurements for the job just completed.
Located and brought to the press: a cart for pulling tools, the power washer, and the coolant vacuum.
Correctly removed all tooling from the press.
Shims are tied to the correct punch, die, or tooling.
All tools unique to the job have been taken to the cleaning station.
All press components are cleaned and brought back to the press.
The press is locked out by all those cleaning the press before cleaning begins.
Press is power washed appropriately (ICOP roller boxes have not been sprayed).
Inside of press and coolant tank have been wiped down and are clean and dry.
Coolant sucker was emptied into the waste oil holding tank.
Floor is mopped and clean.
Outside of press is clean.
Power washer and coolant sucker are clean, ready for next person, and returned to their designated spot.
All tools, equipment, and accessories have been returned to their designated spot.
Team Leader or toolmaker has been notified.

questions.text: List the four main ways in which you can monitor a lubrication system to prevent problems.
answer: Watch for excessively fast or extremely slow oil consumption

questions.text: There is only one type of lubrication system and it is used on all presses.
Any oil can be used in a lubrication system, as long as the reservoir is full.
Lubrication systems provide lubrication for ________, moving parts, and wear surfaces.
Filling reservoirs while the press is down for another reason is a good way to save time and problems, because you don't have to stop and re-start the press.
Oil pressure in a lubrication system should stay in a range of ________ psi.
List three lubrication system contaminants.

checks.statement_en: Given existing presses and lubrication systems, check and refill the oil reservoir and check the oil pressure. Standard tools and lubricants must be used. Standards must match the criteria found on the performance check.

items.text: Correctly identified where the oil reservoir is located on each press.
Each reservoir is at least one-quarter full at all times.
Correctly identified the correct oil to be used in each press.
Correctly located each type of oil in the oil storage area.
Selected, cleaned, and refilled the correct oil can for each press.
Wiped up all spills and overflows.
Used the correct oil for each press.
Did not mix one oil with any others.
Refilled reservoirs during times when the press was down for other reasons.
Oiled rollers on all presses at end-of-shift when requested by toolmaker.
Filled all sight-feed oil cups as required.
Maintained pressure of 20-40 psi on each system.
Took appropriate corrective action when pressure fell below 20 psi.
Moly-coated upper cams and cam rollers on Baird presses after each shift of operation.
Greased each individual grease zerk.
Filters are cleaned and maintained so that they work as intended.

questions.text: What is the proper method of mixing water soluble coolant?
answer: Mix oil into the water while stirring

questions.text: You should always double-check the coolant type before refilling the reservoir.
The coolant ________ direct and adjust the flow of coolant to the tooling.
Coolant does the job of ________ and ________ the tools and material as the press is running.
If coolant is mixed ________ it could lose its properties of lubrication.
Where must used coolant be dumped?
The mixing ratio refers to the amounts of ________ and ________ in the mixture.
How often should you check the coolant level in a press?
answer: As often as every time you walk up to the press

checks.statement_en: Given any press, check, mix, and refill the reservoir so that the reservoir level is constantly maintained. Standards must match the criteria found on the performance check.

items.text: Correctly located the coolant reservoir on each press.
Each reservoir is at least ½ full at all times.
Selected the correct oils to use with each job.
Selected, cleaned, and used the appropriate container for each job.
Correctly mixed the "oil into the water" when mixing coolants.
Mixed coolant to the correct ratio for each job.
Wiped up all spills and overflows.
Refilled reservoirs during times when the press was down for other reasons, when possible.
Disposed of old coolant in the approved, designated location.
Correctly thinned out a thick mixture by first mixing a thin mixture in another container, then mixing into the larger tank.
All containers, tools, and equipment are cleaned and returned to their designated spot after use.

questions.text: Parts coming off the press must be ________ thoroughly before dumping into a 'good parts' barrel.
If you shut down the press for a quality problem, you must immediately ________ the bad parts.
Most rejections are caused by ________ defects.
The first step to performing inspection is to interpret the ________.
A variable gage measures the ________ of a feature, while an ________ gage only checks whether the feature is good or bad.
One of the most important steps after cleaning a height indicator is to ________ the indicator on the base, or the surface you are taking the measurement from.
The accuracy of measuring tools is maintained by performing ________ at regular intervals.
One method of overcoming inspection problems is to ________ them from occurring.
When using effective interpersonal skills, always attack the ________, not the person.
When you can't reach agreement on a quality issue, always ask a ________ to help resolve the problem.
One of the ways to anticipate problems is to look for:

checks.statement_en: Given any inspection tool, can correctly operate the tool to measure the required dimension, to the criteria found on the checklist below.

items.text: Selected the correct size micrometer.
Cleaned the faces and the part to be measured.
Checked zero setting.
Grasped the micrometer correctly, cradling the frame in the palm of the hand.
Adjusted micrometer to approximate size by turning between thumb and forefinger. Did not twirl the micrometer.
Established line of measurement by rocking side to side and over center.
Took a number of readings and noted the high and low readings.
Obtained correct measurement for each pin gage or gage block.
Cleaned the jaws and the part to be measured.
Loosened lock screw and set jaw slightly larger than the dimension to be measured.
Placed the fixed jaw against the reference point of the part.
Rocked the calipers slightly to obtain correct seat of jaws and feel.
Took a number of readings and noted the high and low readings.
Obtained correct measurements for outside, inside, and depth measurements.
Selected the correct tip.
Adjusted the tip so that it is parallel or perpendicular to the work.
Correctly adjusted the height of the indicator to the height of the piece to be measured.
Correctly adjusted to zero using a standard.
Correctly slid the work under the indicator tip.
Took a number of readings and averaged them.
Obtained correct measurement each time.
Selected the correct tip.
Cleaned the indicator and tightened tip.
Cleaned the base, measuring surfaces, and tip.
Zeroed the indicator on the base or the surface being measured from.
Raised the indicator tip and placed it on the surface being measured.
Obtained correct measurement each time.
Selected correct gage, cleaned gage and part.
Correctly applied gage to part (started squarely, did not force or turn).
Correctly identified whether part was good or bad.
Selected correct gage, cleaned gage and part.
Correctly applied gage to part (started squarely, did not force or turn).
Correctly identified whether part was good or bad.
Selected correct gage, cleaned gage and part.
Correctly applied part to gage.
Correctly identified whether part was good or bad.

checks.statement_en: Given any shell, can correctly identify all defects and take appropriate action. Trainer assigns sample box of parts (A–L) and observes defect identification.

items.text: Part A — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part B — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part C — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part D — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part E — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part F — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part G — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part H — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part I — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part J — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part K — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.
Part L — Correctly identified all defects and took appropriate action.

questions.text: The type of material can always be identified by using the ________ sheet.
An overhead crane can be operated by anyone, as long as they operate it safely.
When dumping scrap, always ________ the container before using it.
What four things must you remember each time you dump scrap?
To prevent the possibility of getting cut, you must always do the following:
labels: a. Wear ________ when handling any object.
d. Identify potential cut hazards and ________ them.

questions.text: The main job of stock reel keepers are to:

options.b: Apply outward pressure to the inside of the coil, so the coil won't slip.
options.c: Prevent the outer wraps of the coil from slipping off the coil sideways.
options.d: Create a buffer of material between the stock reel and the press.

questions.text: The correct coil to be used for a job can be identified by matching the coil tag to the production schedule.
When unbanding a coil always remove the ________ bands first.
A coil must always be positioned so that the stock will feed from the ________ of the coil.
The 'Scrap Report' is a record of all the parts ________ at the press.
It is your job to fill out the scrap report at the end of each shift.
The loose end of a coil must always be in the ________ position when unbanding a coil on a stock reel.
If you see a visual defect in the material, immediately tell your supervisor, but leave the press running.

checks.statement_en: Given the situations listed below involving materials, can perform all activities to the criteria found on the checklist below.

items.text: Correctly identified the material type.
Correctly identified the scrap container.
Cleaned the scrap container if required.
Did not mix materials.
Asked for help when dumping scrap if too heavy.
Wore gloves when handling scrap.
Prevented hazards before beginning.
Used the legs, not the back, when lifting.
Asked for assistance if he/she didn't know where to dump scrap.
Followed all safety rules and guidelines.
Successfully located a blank scrap report form.
Completed the Scrap Report at the end of the shift.
All scrapped parts were included in the weigh in.
Correctly weighed the scrapped parts.
All items on the Scrap Report are filled in accurately and correctly.
Asked Quality Assurance for disposition on parts that required it.
Disposed of scrap in the correct scrap container.
Turned in Scrap Report to the correct location.
Possesses a valid crane license before operating the crane.
Possesses a valid Hi-Lo license before operating the Hi-Lo.
Correctly identified the coil or skid of material to be used.
If space between coils was too small for sling, correctly used prybar or Hi-Lo fork with spacer.
Correctly attached sling around coil and onto crane hook.
Picked up coil with a minimum of swinging.
Turned the coil so that the stock will feed from top of coil.
Adjusted mandrel small enough to slip coil onto reel.
Positioned coil against back of mandrel.
Removed sling and manually jogged the mandrel so sling is at bottom of coil (did not catch sling on reel).
Moved crane to a neutral spot.
Expanded mandrel until it holds the internal diameter of the coil securely.
Did not drop the mandrel wrench on the floor.
Slid the keepers on the mandrel arms, positioned against the coil, and tightened snugly.
Positioned the coil so that the loose end is down.
Removed radial banding first, then O.D. bands.
Did not stand in a danger zone when cutting bands.
Cut banding in pieces rather than trying to pull them out.
Wore gloves through the entire coil hanging process.
Operated the stock reel correctly in both manual and automatic mode.
Carefully fed the strip under/through the loop control arm after switching to automatic mode.
Fed the strip correctly through the press (feed rollers, blank, scrap chopper).
Did not restart the press unless trained to do so correctly.
Followed all safety rules and guidelines.

questions.text: A probe system monitors each ________ as it travels through the press.
What four things must you always check before turning a press over?
answer: Is the coolant running into the tooling
Are all the parts in the fingers

questions.text: Turning a press over more than once will not cause problems and can be done at any time.
Preventing problems means that you must visually check at least ten items:

options.b: Each time you walk up to the press

questions.text: The main ingredient in preventing problems is to be ________.
List the ten items you must visually check each time you walk up to the press:
answer: Press area is neat, clean, and organized
Lubrication reservoir has enough oil and oil pressure is normal
Coolant flowing into tooling at same location and velocity
Parts and/or scrap coming out of each chute, staying in tubs, tubs not full
Parts are visually correct — no missing holes, score marks, or smashed parts
Guard doors are closed and everything looks the same as before

questions.text: Your goal is to run ________ parts consistently over a period of time. This means that:

checks.statement_en: Given the situations listed below involving press controls, can perform all activities to the criteria found on the checklist below.

items.text: Press area is neat, clean, and organized.
Lubricant reservoir has oil in it.
Oil pressure is normal.
Coolant reservoir has coolant in it.
Coolant is flowing in same location and with the same velocity as before.
Stock has no visual defects.
Scrap-chopper is operating correctly.
Parts and scrap are coming out of each chute, staying in tubs, tubs are not full.
Parts are visually correct — no missing holes, score marks, or smashed parts.
Guard doors are all closed and everything looks as it did before.
No new noises detected.
Person has completed training to operate the controls of the equipment.
Coolant is running in the correct location and at the correct velocity.
All the parts are in the fingers or accounted for.
Checked if any stations could not be turned over without a part.
Visually checked each station and the press for problems.
Closed all press guards.
Checked entire press area before jogging.
Followed all safety rules.
Correctly operated the probe system.
Jogged the press correctly through one revolution.
Did not turn over the press more times than necessary.
Person has completed training to operate the controls of the equipment.
Coolant is running in the correct location and at the correct velocity.
All the parts are in the fingers or accounted for.
Checked if any stations could not be started without a part.
Visually checked each station and the press for problems.
Closed all press guards and checked entire press area.
Followed all safety rules.
Correctly operated the probe system.
Jogged through one revolution and did not turn over more times than necessary.
Visually checked the tooling and press before running continuous.
Correctly started the press.
Checked the parts after startup.

questions.text: A setup must always be planned, ________, and precise.
Preparing for a setup and the setup itself has a great impact on the total job-run time.
Before running a part in a production mode after a setup, a ________ must be completed:
A process log gives you a method of keeping a ________ of your job.
A setup is not successful unless the job runs consistently over a long term.
Setup time does not include cleaning the press.

checks.statement_en: Given a job to prepare for setup, can locate and interpret the production schedule, job packet, gages and quality paperwork, blueprint, process log records, and setup documentation information, to the standards found on the performance check.

items.text: Located the production schedule.
Correctly interpreted information found on the production schedule.
Located the job packet.
Correctly interpreted information found on the job packet.
Located the quality paperwork.
Correctly interpreted information found on the quality paperwork.
Located the blueprint.
Correctly interpreted information found on the blueprint.
Located the process log records.
Correctly interpreted information found on the process log records.
Located the setup documentation.
Correctly interpreted information found on the setup documentation.

questions.text: The components that are changed for each job can be termed:
What do we call the seven standard pieces of tooling found in most stations?
What are the two major reasons for inspecting tools before a job run?
answer: to maintain a part to blueprint specifications
to maintain a tool within wear specifications

questions.text: Tooling may be replaced or job prints updated, only after approval from my ________.

A transfer block has a correct inside diameter of 1.891 before mounting it in the slide. After it is mounted in the slide, but before the mounting screws are tightened, it maintains an inside diameter of 1.891. After tightening the mounting screws, the inside diameter measures 1.895. Which of the following is true about the transfer block?

options.d: it must be repaired or replaced

questions.text: A stripper removes the part from the die.
A cap screw must enter the tool or component a minimum of ________ times it's diameter.
List two of the four reasons why mating surfaces might not be seating squarely against each other.
When using a set screw to fasten, use a second set screw locked against the first to prevent it from ________.
All nicks, burrs, and gouges must be ________ before assembly.
Where there is more than one fastener, each must be ________ together to give you an even grip.
A transfer block's size is measured (before/after) the transfer block is mounted in the slide and the cap screws tightened.
In addition to the standard assembly procedures, what two conditions must be met each time a transfer block is mounted in the slide?
Each finger for a job will always be marked with a station number.
The two finger springs for each station must be ________.

checks.statement_en: Given a list to tooling components, locate, identify, and explain the purpose of each, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Located/Identified and explained purpose of: Punch
Located/Identified and explained purpose of: Die
Located/Identified and explained purpose of: Thimble
Located/Identified and explained purpose of: Knockout
Located/Identified and explained purpose of: Punch holder
Located/Identified and explained purpose of: Die block
Located/Identified and explained purpose of: Transfer fingers

checks.statement_en: Given a job number, can locate all necessary tooling and components, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Located the correct job check list.
Located the cabinet where the job is stored.
Located all blanking station components.
Located all cupping station components.
Located all drawing station components.
Located all trimming station components.
Located all piercing station components.
Located all beading station components.
Located all side piercing station components.
Identified and located the correct slide.
Identified and located any auxiliary tooling and components.
Located the slide sample used for setup.
Identified any items missing from the above list.

checks.statement_en: Given any tooling and it's blueprint, list any out-of-spec conditions, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Inspected all dimensions.
Identified all "out of specification" occurrences.
Identified all "out of wear tolerance" occurrences.
Correctly identified whether to change the tool, update the job print, or stay the same for each out of spec condition.
Returned all tooling and components to it's appropriate spot.
Identified any other unusual or incorrect conditions.

checks.statement_en: Given any tooling which requires assembly, demonstrate the procedures found on the checksheet, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Fastener enters punch a minimum of 1½ times it's diameter.
Fastener is torqued to specification.
Checked if punch end seats squarely against back end of chuck counterbore.
Checked if mating surfaces seat squarely.
Placed die squarely into die block opening.
Die enters die block smoothly, without excessive hammering.
Used a soft-faced hammer to position die in die block.
Checked if die is seated squarely against shoulder of die block.
Checked if die is flush (within .001) with both top and bottom of die block.
Used dial indicator or "tap" method to align (within .0005) flat on die with the block key slot.
Tightened both screws in the key to torque specification; both screws were tightened simultaneously so that equal pressure is exerted on both.
Checked if die is tight in the die block.
Key fasteners enter die block a minimum of 1½ times it's diameter.
Correct transfer block selected.
Transfer block is free of burrs, nicks, and gouges.
Transfer block is aligned correctly in slide.
Transfer block is flush, within .003 with bottom of slide.
Transfer block mounting screws tighten on shoulder of screw, not the threaded end.
Mounting screws are torqued to specification.
Transfer block is correct size and holds blank tightly without distorting.
Located correct slide.
Located correct fingers.
Located correct slide line-up plate.
Checked transfer slide and fingers for burrs, nicks, gouges, and bends.
Fingers are mounted in correct station.
Finger mounted screws are in and torqued to specification.
Finger springs have correct wire diameter, spring diameter, and spring length.
The ends of each finger spring are crimped shut.
The two finger springs used for each station are identical in wire diameter, spring diameter, spring length, and tension.
All springs are in. The set screws are adjusted so that the correct tension is put on the shell and neither the springs or the fingers bottom out when the finger is opened.
Fingers have full and complete movement.
Finger location has been set with the slide line-up plate.
Fingers grip the shell correctly with the slide setup plug in place.
Fingers do not stick on die blocks or line-up plate when opened.
Nib seats squarely against pilot should.
Nib seats squarely against punch end.
Fastener enters punch a minimum of 1½ times it's diameter.
Nib should seats squarely against retainer.
Checked for radius on back edge of nib.
Checked for outside diameter radius on end of retainer.
Checked cutting edge for sharpness and edge type.
Fastener enters retainer a minimum of 1½ times it's diameter.
Placed die squarely into opening.
Die enters retainer smoothly, without excessive hammering.
Used a soft-faced hammer to position die.
Checked if die is seated squarely against shoulder of retainer.
Checked if die sticks out of bottom of retainer.
Key screws enter retainer a minimum of 1½ times their diameter.
Key screws are tightened to torque specification.
Checked if die is tight in the retainer, after tightening screws.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pilot and punch
items.text: Selected spring of the correct length, wire diameter, and spring diameter.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pilot and punch
items.text: Selected the correct size roll pin.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pilot and punch
items.text: Tapped the pin into the hole with the flat on the pilot in the correct position.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pilot and punch
items.text: Assembled pin, spring, and set screw so that the pin has the correct position.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pilot and punch
items.text: Ground the ends of the roll pin flush with the punch diameter without damaging the punch.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pilot and punch
items.text: Pilot operates freely within the punch.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pin into punch
items.text: Selected pin of the correct size and type.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pin into punch
items.text: Selected spring of the correct length, wire diameter, and spring diameter.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pin into punch
items.text: Ground pin to correct length.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pin into punch
items.text: Assembled pin, spring, and set screw so that the pin has the correct tension on it.

items.section: Assemble spring loaded pin into punch
items.text: Tightened second set screw against the first so that the first set screw does not back off.

items.section: Assemble flange trim die into die block
items.text: Correctly calculated the thickness of the spacer ring.

items.section: Assemble flange trim die into die block
items.text: Ground spacer ring to calculated thickness.

items.section: Assemble flange trim die into die block
items.text: Placed ring on die and placed die squarely into die block opening. Die enters retainer smoothly, without excessive hammering.

items.section: Assemble flange trim die into die block
items.text: Used a soft-faced hammer to position die in die block.

checks.statement_en: Given any job, lay out all tooling and components on the job cart to the standards found on the check sheet.

items.text: The job cart used is the correct one for the machine or job being set up.
Correct slide line-up plate is on cart.
Correct job order is on cart.
Correct job check list is on cart.
Correct pages and quality paperwork are on cart.
Correct documentation sheet is on cart.
Correct punch assemblies are on cart, in proper station sequence.
Correct die assemblies are on cart, in proper station sequence.
Correct slide samples are on cart.
Correct production print is on cart.
Side motion components are on cart.
Correct trim bracket assembly is on cart.
All tooling and components found on the job check list are on cart.

questions.text: Briefly describe the function of each of the following components.
answer: House plungers in specific locations - upper and lower
Feeds the stock through the machine
Provides back and forth motion to the slide
Monitors the transfer of parts between stations
Provides fine adjustment of punch length in an ICOP
Connects plunger and lifter rod together in an ICOP
Transmits motion from wedge to roller bearing
Provides a bearing surface between the plunger and the plunger cam

questions.text: The model number 15-8 means:

options.c: Nothing. It is just an insignificant number to designate the press.
options.d: There are 15 plungers which are size 8.

questions.text: The name ICOP means "Individual cam operated plunger".
On an ICOP, the motor and clutch drive the back shaft, which drives the top shaft, which drives the side shaft, which drives the bottom shaft.
On any transfer press, one revolution of the press is more than one revolution of the bottom shaft, depending on the speed.
What three problems are caused by a bumper not being flush?
answer: Pull out hold down bolts for the die block.
Knockout remains below die surface - parts tip over.
Knockout cam turns on bottom shaft.

questions.text: In timing an ICOP, the general rule is:

options.d: Timing has nothing to do with the height of the plunger

questions.text: The limits to which you can time a plunger include which of the following:

options.a: So fast that the roller locks up on the plunger cam.
options.b: So slow that the part coming into the station gets tipped over.

questions.text: The first lift dwells the part so that the ________ can pick up the part.
In the model number 3-25, the 25 refers to the frame size.
A Baird uses a series of individual plungers.
The model number 814 tells us that there is approximately ________ tons per station.

checks.statement_en: Given a list of press components for an ICOP, a Platarg, and a Baird press, locate, identify, and explain the purpose for each, at the press, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - locate, identify, and explain purpose
items.text: Upper Cam Shaft

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - locate, identify, and explain purpose
items.text: Gibs

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - locate, identify, and explain purpose
items.text: Stripper delay

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - locate, identify, and explain purpose
items.text: Roll feed

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - locate, identify, and explain purpose
items.text: Wedge

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - locate, identify, and explain purpose
items.text: Lifter arm/bar

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - locate, identify, and explain purpose
items.text: Roller bearing

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - locate, identify, and explain purpose
items.text: Kicker

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - locate, identify, and explain purpose
items.text: Pressure pad linkage

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - find and explain (tell the name)
items.text: Side shaft

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - find and explain (tell the name)
items.text: Bottom shaft

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - find and explain (tell the name)
items.text: Scrap chopper

items.section: Waterbury Farrel ICOP/Platarg - find and explain (tell the name)

checks.statement_en: Given a Waterbury Farrel ICOP or a Platarg with adjustable timing, can demonstrate and explain timing requirements and adjustments to the standards found on the checksheet, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Explained the operation of the press. (Motor and clutch, backshaft, topshaft, side shaft, and bottom shaft)
Demonstrated and identified "downstroke", "first lift", "transfer back", "second lift", and "transfer forward" as they barred the press over.
Timed a station to its slowest timing. Part coming in is not knocked over.
Timed a station to its fastest timing. Station does not lock up.
Completed all the above steps safely and without incident. Recognized all danger points to self, tooling, press components, and press.
Returned all tools and equipment to the appropriate spot.
Left press clean and in good condition.

checks.statement_en: Given any job in an ICOP, Platarg, or Baird press, can adjust press components to meet standards described on check sheet.

items.text: Can correctly name the components of the assembly: bumper rod, bumper nib, bumper friction, bumper bushing (top and bottom), bumper bushing plate, kicker, knockout cam.
Checked for and removed burrs.
Cleaned hole and bumper assembly.
Correctly inserted and mounted bumper bushings.
Correctly mounted kicker.
Mounted cam so that rounded portion leads.
Correctly inserted and mounted bumper.
Correctly inserted and adjusted bumper friction.
Jogged press to correct degree position to adjust timing of knockout cam.
Timed cam so that flat spot is straight up and down.
Tightened cam bolts simultaneously and checked for even gap.
Checked if knockout cam was in fully up position.
Pushed down on bumper and checked if bumper was flush with bed.
Changed washers or re-ground bumper nib to achieve flush status.
Checked for and removed burrs from die block or bed.
Wiped bed and die block clean.
Pushed bumper rod down far enough to allow knockout to drop into bumper hole (approximately 1/2 inch).
Die block assemblies are placed in correct station.
Snugged down front die bolt.
Returned all tools and components to correct location.
Can correctly name the components of the bumper assembly: bumper rod, bumper rod end, bumper spring, bumper mounting plate, kicker, knockout cam.
Checked for and removed burrs.
Cleaned hole and bumper assembly.
Correctly inserted and mounted bumper assembly.
Tested spring tension and movement of bumper.
Adjusted bumper rod end flush with bed.
Tightened lock nut against adjustment nut without changing adjustment.
Mounted kicker so that adjustment screw is on center with bumper rod.
Mounted knockout cam so that the cam fall-off occurs after the cam reaches top dead center.
Jogged machine to correct position to time cam.
Adjusted cam to top dead center and tightened.
Adjusted kicker adjustment bolt upward until it contacts the bumper rod and tightened lock nut.
In the position given in "12", the kicker does not lock up.
Explained what will happened if the kicker is adjusted during the run.
Slide slipped in freely.
Inserted and tighten pin or screw.
Installed slide friction. Tension is set so the slide slap is eliminated. Friction is neither over or under tightened.
Installed and adjusted front slide guide correctly.
Backed off adjustment screw on front slide guide.
Applied layout dye to end of blank punch.
Threaded blank punch into plunger and tightened.
Barred punch downward into transfer block and back up after checking rough alignment.
Determined if punch lined up with transfer block and communicated problem to Supervisor.

questions.text: The proper way to back off a roller box is listed in this module. Give a brief explanation of this procedure.
answer: Loosen the roller box locking screw and pry the roller box down until it contacts the top of the plunger, then tighten the locking screw

questions.text: List the two reasons for leaving a two material thickness space between the blank punch and the bed in the fully down position.
answer: In case of a double blank
So the blank does not scrape the bed during transfer

questions.text: What must you be careful for when tightening up an adjustment wedge lock screw?

options.d: The wedge moving as you tighten it

questions.text: The reason for using parallels during the alignment process is that it allows you to:

options.a: Have your hands free to hold the shims
options.b: Align the blank holder with the bed

questions.text: The visual alignment method should be used whenever possible.
What thickness shims would you use to align a blank which has .002 total clearance?
The visual method of alignment relies on your ability to create equal clearance all the way around the punch and die by sight.
The best way to double check alignment is to use layout dye and look for ________ on the nib diameter.
List the four characteristics that must be present after you make and inspect your first blank:
answer: Held securely by the transfer block
Free of defects like nicks, scratches, and burrs
Has an even break around its edge
Delivered to the next station without interruption of operation

checks.statement_en: Given a cross-section of a blank station, can identify each component by name, with 100 percent accuracy. Refer to the cross-section for an ICOP/Platarg press and identify the components labeled A through I.

items.text: A — Setscrew
B — Blank retainer
C — Ejector pin spring
D — Blank nib
E — Retainer bolt
F — Ejector pin
G — Stripper plate
H — Blank die
I — Transfer block

checks.statement_en: Given any blank punch and die, can sharpen them to the standards found on the performance check, with 100 percent accuracy. The trainee must sharpen the tooling listed below.

items.text: Obtained correct grinding fixture.
Cleaned die and fixture.
Correctly located die in fixture.
Tightened mounting screws securely. Die is tight.
Mounted fixture in grinder so that die shear angle runs side-to-side and the fixture is tipped backward.
Removed all nicks or shear marks.
Shear angles meet on die center.
No bump is formed where shear angles meet.
Removed die from fixture.
Demagnetized fixture and die.
Removed burr from cutting edge.
Radiused the top outside edge where the top of the die meets the outside angle of the die life.
Cleaned tools thoroughly of all dust and dirt.
Stored tools in correct location.
Obtained correct grinding fixture.
Cleaned punch and fixture.
Tightened punch into fixture so that punch will not loosen during grinding.
Blocked up fixture on grinder.
Removed enough material during grinding operation to eliminate any nicks or shear marks.
Demagnetized punch and fixture.
Ground a 20° X 1/8 inch angle on the punch on jobs where the material is .015 thick or less.
Cleaned tools thoroughly.
Stored tools in correct location.
Obtained angle plate and two clamps.
Cleaned angle plate and punch.
Set the angle plate on its side on the magnetic chuck.
Set the backside of the blank punch against the angle plate.
Turned on the magnet and clamped the blank punch to the angle plate.
Turned off the magnet and tipped the blank punch up to grind the top surface.
Ground first .0005 to determine if punch was being ground square. (Checked and/or realigned if it was not.)
Ground the cutting edge to remove all shear marks and/or chips.
Demagnetized punch, angle plate and clamps.
Lapped the end of the punch using at most a medium lapping compound.
Removed burr from cutting edge.
Cleaned tools thoroughly.
Stored tools in correct location.

checks.statement_en: Given a blank punch, blank die, and a stripper plate, mount and align them so that the blank has an even break, is held securely by the transfer block, is delivered to the next station without interruption of operation, and is free from defects, with 100 percent accuracy. As the trainee mounts a blank station in both a Baird and an ICOP press, check each item successfully completed.

items.text: Cleaned shoulder and threads of chuck and the inside of the plunger of all dirt and foreign material.
Using left hand to hold the punch against plunger, threaded the punch into the plunger and tightened it to torque specifications.
Checked if wedge is completely backed out. Loosened roller box locking screw and pried roller box down.
Double-checked blank punch length to make sure punch will not bottom out in the fully down position.
Barred punch to fully down position.
Loosened up lifter bar.
Tapped in wedge to create two to three material thicknesses between bottom of punch and bed.

Loosened lifter bar and created a gap of about 1/16 - 1/8 of an inch between the lifter bar and the lifter cam. (While maintaining the two to three material thicknesses between punch and bed and plunger roller is contacting the plunger cam.) Re-tightened lifter bar.
Checked to make sure that the roller bearing does not lock up with the plunger cam at several points within one machine revolution.
Double-checked all adjustments and the tightness of all bolts and components.
Returned all tools to correct location.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Checked for and removed burrs from press and tooling.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Wiped press and tools clean of dirt.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Mounted blank punch correctly (see competency check #4).

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Removed blank punch, inserted punch in die, slid entire unit in press. Threaded blank punch back up into plunger.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Barred press over into the position where the blank punch is 1/8 of an inch above blank die.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Placed two, 3/8 to 1/2 inch parallels under blank die retainer so that blank punch is inside of die.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Barred blank punch down approximately 1/2 inch, and removed parallels - blank punch is still inside die.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Threaded hold down bolts into retainer, leaving them finger tight.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Lined up punch and die using appropriate method.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Double-checked alignment using layout dye. No rub marks are visible.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Mounted blank stripper plate, centered around punch.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Inserted material and produced a blank.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Blank is free of defects, has an even break, and is held securely by transfer block.

items.section: ICOP/Platarg — Mount and Align Blank Tools
items.text: Blank is delivered to the next station with no interruption of operation.
Checked for and removed burrs from press and tooling.
Wiped press and tools clean of dirt.
Assembled blank punch, cup punch, bracket, and clamps correctly.
Slid blank die retainer assembly onto press, inserted hold-down bolts leaving them finger tight.
Mounted blank punch assembly correctly.
Tightened bracket bolts to torque specification while holding it in it's fully up position.
Mounted front cup arm dog and tightened bolt to torque specification.
Backed off adjustment screw, and held punch fully up while snugging up strap bolts.
Barred ram downward until punch is approximately 1/4 inch above die.
Loosened punch strap bolts and gently lowered blank punch into die.
Barred ram downward to bottom dead center.
Placing a thickness gage the same thickness as the material between the top of the blank punch and the bottom of the adjustment screw, adjusted the punch downward until it contacts bottom.
Tightened the adjustment screw lock screw.
Lifted blank punch fully up and tightened strap bolts, starting with lower right bolt.
Aligned punch and die using appropriate method.
Double-checked alignment using layout dye. No rub marks are visible.
Mounted stripper plate, centered around punch.
Inserted material and produced a blank.
Blank is free of defects, has an even break.
Cupped shell matches slide sample and is held securely by the fingers.
Cup is delivered to the next station with no interruption of operation.

checks.statement_en: Given a standard roll feed unit, adjust throw so that the bridge between each blank is less than 1/16 of an inch, yet does not cut out, with 100 percent accuracy. Assign the trainee to adjust the feed on a standard roll feed for jobs being set up at the current time.

items.text: Jogged the press to the correct location to adjust the roll feed.
Loosened lock nut and adjusted arm in correct direction.
Bridge is less than 1/16 inch wide, without cutting out.
Checked if feed rolls are placing appropriate tension on stock. Used "shears" method to measure drag.
Checked if material is being delivered straight into the chopper, without buckling.
Adjusted feed roll tension correctly to eliminate improper tension and feeding off center into chopper.
Checked brake adjustment and adjusted the brake as required.
Bridge width does not vary from blank to blank.

checks.statement_en: Given 10 problems in a blank station, can identify the cause of the problem and the possible solutions for each, with 100 percent accuracy. For each of the following problems, list all possible causes and the solutions for each. (Refer to 'Correct Blank Station Problems' in the lesson for the correct answers.)

items.text: Problem 1: Blank is cut out — identified all possible causes (a–f) and the solutions for each.
Problem 2: Blank produces shavings — identified all possible causes (a–f) and the solutions for each.
Problem 3: Bridge varies in width — identified all possible causes (a–f) and the solutions for each.
Problem 4: Blank pulls up — identified all possible causes (a–c) and the solutions for each.
Problem 5: Blank loose in transfer block — identified all possible causes (a–d) and the solutions for each.
Problem 6: Scrap buckles in front of chopper — identified all possible causes (a–e) and the solutions for each.
Problem 7: Uneven break around edge of blank — identified all possible causes (a–c) and the solutions for each.
Problem 8: Material does not feed forward — identified all possible causes (a–c) and the solutions for each.
Problem 9: Blank slips under slide — identified all possible causes (a–b) and the solutions for each.
Problem 10: Blank has scratch marks on its surface — identified all possible causes (a–c) and the solutions for each.

questions.text: Name the two adjustments that can be made with the pressure pad adjustment linkage:
Describe the spot where the pressure on the blank is correct: ________
answer: just enough to remove the wrinkles

questions.text: List the steps involved in shimming a punch:
answer: Bar or jog the punch downward until it enters the shell.
Loosen the chuck and place the proper thickness shim behind the chuck in a location which, after tightening the chuck securely, will center the punch within the shell.
Loosen the die and jog the shell down into it for proper alignment. Tighten the die bolts.
Repeat this process until the shell has uniform flange, radii, and draw rings.

questions.text: The stripper dogs used for the pressure pad are identical to those used for a standard stripper.
The pressure exerted on the blank comes from the pressure pad. This pressure is changed by adjusting the:
The punch should be two to three material thicknesses above the die surface at the first lift position.
The knockout cam must be adjusted so that the knockout is fully up in the first lift position.
Which way do you turn the stop collar to lower the stripper height adjustment for the pressure pad?
After making one cup, you can run the press through one revolution to make another shell, without worrying about the operation of the cup station.
The pressure exerted on the blank during the cupping operation must be just enough to remove the ________.
Shimming the punch is needed because the punch does not line up with the fingers.

checks.statement_en: Given a cross-section of a cup station, name each component and explain its function, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Identified the chuck or punch holder.
Identified the punch.
Identified the pressure pad.
Identified the cup die.
Identified the knockout.
Identified the pressure pad linkage (pressure pad/arm, lower arm, and linkage adjustment screw).
Explained the function of each cup station component.

checks.statement_en: Given the cup station tools for one job, in an ICOP press, mount, align, and adjust each component so that the criteria on the check sheet are met, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Checked for and removed burrs from tooling and press.
Wiped tooling and plunger and cleaned of dirt and foreign material.
Inserted dogs (with smaller diameter and shorter length shanks than standard) into the pressure pad arm.
Inserted pressure pad into pressure pad arm opening and onto dogs.
Inserted punch into pressure pad, threaded punch into plunger, and tightened securely.
Backed off stripper adjustment and pressure adjustment.
Jogged slide to forward position. Pushed down on pressure pad arm to determine if pressure pad moves freely into transfer block.
Jogged punch down to about 1/4 inch above die.
Loosened front die bolt and pried punch into die.
Jogged punch to bottom of stroke. Snugged front die bolt.
Using a strip of raw material or feeler gage of the same thickness, adjusted pressure exerted by pressure pad on blank. Tightened lock nuts securely.
Jogged punch to first lift position. Double-checked to make sure pressure pad would clear first pair of fingers.
Loosened lifter bar and adjusted punch timing so punch is two to three material thicknesses off die surface. Lifter bar is centered on lifter cam. Lifter bar bolts are tightened securely.
Double-checked knockout cam timing - knockout should be two to three material thicknesses below die surface.
Jogged press to stripper delay position. Adjusted stripper height using slide sample of this station. Tightened lock nut securely.
Jogged press to top of stroke, with slide in forward position, and a blank in the transfer block. Loosened up die block and jogged shell into the die. Tightened up die block securely.
Jogged punch back up to top very slowly, double-checking operation of station.
Inspected shell for length, concentricity of radii, appearance and made adjustments to match it to slide sample.
Pressure is adjusted to remove wrinkles but not to exert more pressure than is needed to remove wrinkles.
Shell is gripped firmly by fingers and transfers to next station without interruption of operation.
Double-checked all adjustments and the tightness of all bolts.

checks.statement_en: Given nine problems in a cup station, identify the cause of each and the possible solutions, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Identified causes and solutions for: Wrinkles in the flange.
Identified causes and solutions for: Shell radii larger on one side than the other.
Identified causes and solutions for: Bulge in side wall.
Identified causes and solutions for: Shell tips over as slide moves forward.
Identified causes and solutions for: Score marks on shell.
Identified causes and solutions for: Cut-out in flange.
Identified causes and solutions for: Flange is eared.
Identified causes and solutions for: Flange is larger on one side than the other.
Identified causes and solutions for: Cup wall is too thin.

questions.text: List the five types of draw stations.
List the two reasons for using a spring sleeve in a draw station.
answer: aligns the shell on the punch

questions.text: With a bottoming knockout, adjusting the punch too deeply will cause the punch to ____.
The nest is used to provide:
A draw punch must be ____ material thicknesses off die surface in the ____ lift position.
The stripper height must be set in the stripper delay position. The shell must be carried about ____ off die surface.
If a spring sleeve is used, the punch may need to be timed more slowly. Where does the punch get adjusted on first lift if this occurs?
Given the cross-section of a draw/redraw station, identify each labeled component (A through F) by name.

checks.statement_en: Given the draw/redraw station tools for each draw situation, mount, align, and adjust each tool or component so that the criteria on the checksheet are met, with 100 percent accuracy. As the trainee mounts the tools for both a draw station and a redraw station (which uses a spring sleeve), place a check next to each item successfully completed by the trainee.

items.text: Cleaned shoulder and threads of chuck and the inside of the plunger of all dirt and foreign material.
Inserted stripper dogs into stripper arm.
Backed off stripper adjustment screw.
Inserted punch into stripper, threaded punch into plunger, and tightened securely.
Jogged punch down to within 1/4 inch of die surface.
Loosened front bolt.
Pried punch into die. Snugged front die bolt.
Jogged press to first lift position.
Loosened lifter bar. Adjusted punch height to create a two to three material thickness gap between the bottom of the punch and the top of the die.
Adjusted knockout cam so that the knockout is two to three material thicknesses below die surface.
Centered lifter bar on lifter cam and tightened bolts securely.
Jogged press to stripper delay position.
Using the slide sample from the station, adjusted the stripper height so that the shell is carried about 1/32 off die surface. Tightened lock nut securely.
Inserted shell into finger, loosened front die bolt, and jogged shell into the die, stopping on bottom. Tightened front die bolt securely.
Jogged press to top of stroke. Checked shell length and adjusted wedge to create correct shell length. Jogged one more shell through the station.
Drawn shell is free of score marks, rub marks, shavings, and bulges. Radii are concentric.
Shell is the same length as the slide sample and is held securely by the fingers.
Shell is delivered to the next station with no interruption of operation.
Double-checked all adjustments and the tightness of all bolts.

questions.text: Wrinkles in the flange: list the possible causes.
answer: shell coming in is too short

questions.text: Shell radii larger on one side than the other: list the possible causes.
Bulge in side wall of shell / collapsed shell bottom (caused by timing/adjustment of components): list the possible solutions.
Shell tips over as slide moves forward: list the possible causes.
answer: fingers do not grip shell properly

questions.text: Score marks on shell: list the possible causes.
answer: shell bottom going in is scored

questions.text: Cut-out in flange: list the possible causes.
Flange is eared: list the possible causes.
Flange is larger on one side than the other: list the possible causes.
answer: fingers do not line up with tooling

questions.text: Shell wall is too thin: list the possible causes.
answer: previous flange being pulled into die

questions.text: The two types of trim stations are:
The four types of scrap removal devices are:
The timing of the knockout cam for a flange trim must be ________ than for a pinch trim.
What four things can cause a sheared cutting edge.
answer: Misalignment of the punch and die
Shell tipping over and crunching between punch and die

checks.statement_en: Given a cross-section of a trim station, can identify each component by name, with 100 percent accuracy. Refer to the cross-section of a trim station and identify each of the components labeled A through H.

items.text: A — retainer bolt
B — chuck
C — trim punch
D — stripper plate
E — trim nib
F — trim pilot
G — retainer bolt
H — trim die

checks.statement_en: Given the tools for a trim station, sharpen each to the standards found on the checksheet, with 100 percent accuracy. Observe the trainee as they sharpen trim station tools.

items.text: Removed insert from punch.
Removed burrs from insert O.D.
Kept insert square while grinding.
Sharpened insert correctly.
All shear is removed.
Removed burr from sharpened edge.
Correctly placed .005 radius on insert when appropriate.
Correctly reassembled punch.
Set up die correctly on grinder.
Sharpened die correctly.
All shear is removed in .015 or .030 increments.
Removed burr from cutting edge.
Cut back shoulder of die if appropriate.
Correctly reassembled die.

checks.statement_en: Given the tooling for both a flange and a pinch trim in both a Baird and an ICOP/Platarg press, can mount, align, and adjust each component so that the criteria on the check sheet are met, with 100 percent accuracy. Observe the trainee as they mount, align, and adjust a trim station in a press.

items.text: Cleaned shoulder and threads of chuck and the inside of the plunger of all dirt and foreign material.
Removed stripper arm.
Inserted punch into plunger and tightened securely.
Jogged punch down to within 1/4 inch of die surface.
Loosened front die bolt.

Pryed punch downward until cutting edges are about 1/32 of an inch apart. Loosened the chuck and turned the punch into the die without shearing the cutting edges. Pryed punch fully down and tightened chuck securely. Punch/insert is still in die.
Aligned punch and die either with shims or visual method. Tightened both front and back die bolts securely.
Double-checked alignment using layout dye.
Adjusted punch depth so that it enters die approximately 1/16 of an inch with the plunger on bottom.
Jogged press to the point where the fingers have cleared the trim ring.
Loosened lifter bar and adjusted punch to the point where the cutting edge is in the fully down position.
Centered lifter bar on the lifter cam and tightened it securely.
Loosened knockout cam and backed it off. Snugged up both bolts.
Jogged punch to the top of the stroke. Mount stripper plate, leaving it loose.
Pryed punch into opening and jogged punch to the bottom of it's stroke.
Lifted stripper plate to within 1/8 inch of contacting the bottom of the chuck. Centered the opening around the punch and tightened securely.
Mounted blower so that the blower pipe is slightly under and centered on the stripper plate. Tightened securely. Jogged press to top of stroke.

Pinch trim - inserted a shell and jogged the punch to the point where the fingers are fully back. Loosened the knockout cam and adjusted it so that the part will just begin exiting the die. Flange trim - inserted a shell and jogged the punch to a point where the fingers will clear the trim ring. Loosened the knockout cam and adjusted it so that the part will follow the punch up and out of the die, before the next fingers begin touching the part.
Jogged one or more shell through the station to make final adjustments and double check operation of the station.
Trimmed shell is free of score marks, shavings, bulges, and burrs; edge of trim has even break.
Shell is the same length as the slide sample and is held securely by the fingers.
Shell is delivered to the next station with no interruption of operation.
Double-checked all adjustments and the tightness of all components.

checks.statement_en: Given a list of problems in a trim station, can identify the possible causes and solutions for each, with 100 percent accuracy. For each of the following problems, identify all possible causes and the solutions for each. (Refer to 'Correct Trim Station Problems' in the lesson for the correct answers.)

items.text: Problem 1: Flange shell has bent flange — identified all possible causes (a–b) and the solutions for each.
Problem 2: Wrinkled or cut-out flange — identified all possible causes (a–b) and the solutions for each.
Problem 3: Bulge in side — identified all possible causes (a–b) and the solutions for each.
Problem 4: Shell tips over as slide moves forward — identified all possible causes (a–b) and the solutions for each.
Problem 5: Pinch trimmed shell pulls up — identified all possible causes (a–b) and the solutions for each.
Problem 6: Sheared cutting edges — identified all possible causes (a–c) and the solutions for each.
Problem 7: Trimmed shell catches under fingers — identified all possible causes (a–b) and the solutions for each.
Problem 8: Trim rings dropping — identified all possible causes (a–d) and the solutions for each.

questions.text: The two types of pierce stations are:
The pierce is timed so that the fingers are fully ________ before the punch begins moving upward.
What is the best method of putting the punch into the die when aligning a round pierce?

options.c: loosen chuck and turn punch into die

questions.text: What are the sharpening standards for full and partial bottom pierces? Give the standard for the punch and the die of each.

checks.statement_en: Given a cross section of a pierce station, can identify each component by name and explain its function, with 100 percent accuracy. Refer to the cross-section of a pierce station and identify the components labeled A through G.

items.text: A — Retainer bolt
B — Chuck
C — Pierce punch
D — Stripper
E — Pierce die
F — Pierce die retainer
G — Filler block

checks.statement_en: Given the tools for a pierce station, sharpen each to the standards found on the checksheet, with 100 percent accuracy. Observe the trainee as they sharpen pierce station tools. Place a check next to each item successfully completed by the trainee.

items.text: Removed punch from punch holder.
Kept punch square while grinding.
Sharpened punch correctly.
All shear is removed.
Removed burr from sharpened edge.
Correctly placed .005 radius or angle when appropriate.
Correctly reassembled punch.
Set up die correctly on grinder.
Sharpened die correctly.
All shear is removed in .015 or .030 increments.
Removed burr from cutting edge.
Cut back shoulder of die if appropriate.
Correctly reassembled die.

checks.statement_en: Given the tools for both a full bottom and partial bottom pierce, in a transfer press, mount, align, and adjust each component so that the criteria on the check sheet are met, with 100 percent accuracy. As the trainee mounts both a full bottom and a partial bottom pierce station, place a check next to each item successfully completed by the trainee.

items.text: Cleaned shoulder and threads of punch holder and the inside of the plunger of all dirt and foreign material.
Removed stripper arm.
Inserted punch into plunger and tightened securely.
Barred punch down to within 1/4 inch of the surface.
Loosened front die bolt.

Pryed punch downward until cutting edges are about 1/32 of an inch apart. Loosened the chuck and turned the punch into the die without shearing the cutting edges. Pryed punch fully down and tightened chuck securely. Punch/lob is still in die.
Aligned punch and die either with shims or visual method. Tightened both front and back die bolts securely.
Double-checked alignment using layout dye.
Jogged punch to the fully down position and adjusted the punch so that it enters die about 1/16 of an inch.
Barred press to the point where the fingers are fully back.
Loosened lifter bar and adjusted punch to the point where the cutting edge is in the fully down position.
Centered lifter bar on the lifter cam and tightened it securely.
Jogged punch to the top of the stroke. Mounted stripper plate, leaving it loose.
Pryed punch into opening and jogged punch to the bottom of it's stroke.
Using a slide sample, adjusted stripper plate to strip shell 1/32 off die surface. Centered the opening around the punch and tightened securely.
Jogged one more shell through the station to make final adjustments and double check operation of the station.
Pierced shell is free of score marks, shavings, bulges, and burrs; edge of pierce has even break.
Shell is the same length as the slide sample and is held securely by the fingers.
Shell is delivered to the next station with no interruption of operation.
Double-checked all adjustments and the tightness of all components.

checks.statement_en: Given four problems in a pierce station, can identify the possible causes and solutions for each, with 100 percent accuracy. For each of the following problems, identify all possible causes and the solution for each. (Refer to 'Correct Piercing Station Problems' in the lesson for the correct answers.)

items.text: Problem 1: Bulge in side — identified all possible causes (a–f) and the solution for each.
Problem 2: Shell tips over as slide moves forward — identified all possible causes (a–b) and the solution for each.
Problem 3: Sheared cutting edges — identified all possible causes (a–c) and the solution for each.
Problem 4: Slugs pulling up — identified all possible causes (a–e) and the solution for each.

questions.text: The probe feeler wire is the only thing that may touch.
If any part of the probe assembly other than the feeler comes into contact with something, the probe will ________ out.
To make sure the probe is hitting only the part, jog an empty ________ through the press.
A probe must be adjusted, not ________.
What is the purpose of a mini-test-run?

options.a: To break in new tooling before production
options.b: To determine if the press is ready to run and the shell is in spec
options.c: To warm up the press lubrication system
options.d: To count the number of spare tools needed

questions.text: You should give a first press approval only after you are confident the press will run ________ parts on a ________ basis.

checks.statement_en: Given a cross section of a probe, can identify each component by name, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: A - feeler and arm assembly
B - lockscrew
C - probe head
D - fiber insert
E - probe adaptor
F - lockscrew
G - probe sleeve
H - probe bracket

checks.statement_en: Given the probes for one job, mount and adjust each one so that the criteria on the checksheet are met, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Correctly assembled each probe.
The slot in the fiber insert is pointed up.
Correctly mounted probes in press.
Probe feeler wires are centered between stations.
Probe feeler wires are centered between fingers.
Probe feeler wires are set to correct height.
No part of the probe touches anything around it.
Checked all banana plugs for tightness.
Checked the sequence of probes by touching one end of the probe wire to a ground.
All lights on the panel work.
Checked to make sure probes do not touch anything but the shell.
All probes hit simultaneously.
With the press running, checked to make sure the press shuts off if a probe wire is pulled.

checks.statement_en: Given a list of problems with a probe system, can identify the possible causes and solutions for each, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.section: Indicator light does not go on
items.text: Identified possible causes (burnt out bulb; probe not touching shell; bad probe wire; probe wire not tight in probe bar or probe adaptor; bad relay) and solutions (replace bulb; re-adjust probe / find out where shell went; replace probe wire; expand banana plugs; replace relay).

Identified possible causes (probe grounding out; probe wire grounding out; bad relay) and solutions (clear shavings / re-adjust probe; move the wire so it does not cross another wire or touch a press part; replace relay).

items.section: Indicator lights do not go on together
items.text: Identified possible cause (probes not timed together) and solution (adjust probes to contact each shell simultaneously).

items.section: Press shuts off when there is nothing mechanically wrong with it
items.text: Identified possible causes (bad relay; probe timing is off; banana plug tightness; cam actuated limit switches timed incorrectly) and solutions (replace relay; re-adjust probes; expand plug ends; contact maintenance department).

items.section: Press continues running after probe wire is pulled
items.text: Identified possible causes (bad relay; bad master relay; poorly adjusted cam-actuated limit switches) and solutions (replace relay; replace relay; contact maintenance department).

checks.statement_en: Given a job that has been set up, complete the activities needed to run the job automatically and perform a mini-test run, so that the criteria on the check sheet are met, with 100 percent accuracy.

items.text: Double-check tightness of all bolts.
Attached and/or closed all press guards.
Turned coolant on.
Adjusted shell to specification.
Ran press to look for problems.
Checked probe system operation (pulled probe wires).
Checked press guarding operation.
Solved all problems as they occurred.
Performed first piece approval only after being assured that the press would run quality parts on a consistent basis.
Did not walk away from the press after starting it up.
Communicated status of press to press operators and floor inspectors.
Double-checked part quality.

checks.statement_en: Given any job, perform a complete job setup to meet requirements found on checksheet.

items.text: Obtained and distributed job order.
Checked if all necessary gages and quality records are laid out.
Identified shell and job characteristics.
Interpreted layout sheet.
Analyzed process log records and list any special and recurring problems.
Eliminated all problems before setup.
Located all necessary tooling and components.
Inspected all tooling for out-of-spec conditions.
Assembled all tooling appropriately.
Assembled transfer block and fingers appropriately in slide.
Layed out all tools and components appropriately on cart.
Used setup documentation sheets to adjust press components to standard for the job.
Positioned all the block assemblies.
Mounted slide motion assembly.
Mounted and adjusted slide.
Mounted the blank punch.
Mounted and aligned the blank tools.
Adjusted the roll or stagger feed.
Mounted and adjusted cup tools.
Mounted and adjusted draw/redraw tools.
Mounted and adjusted trim tools.
Mounted and adjusted pierce tools.
Mounted and adjusted bead tools.
Mounted and adjusted side pierce tools.
Mounted and adjusted lance tools.
Mounted and adjusted roll and arbor station.
Mounted and adjusted any specialty stations.
Mounted and adjusted all probes correctly.
Performed mini-test-run.
Solved all problems as they occurred.
Performed first piece approval.
Communicated status of press to press operators and floor inspectors.
Parts meet quality standards.
